results.possibleIdentity: Scotty Snyder (born c. 1912) was an American artist and performer active in the New York avant-garde scene from the 1960s through the 1980s. She is historically significant for her association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg; records indicate she participated in E.A.T. exhibition programming between November 1968 and February 1969. Snyder was a frequent collaborator and subject for the performance artist Stuart Sherman, appearing in his films and performance documentations such as "Scotty Snyder (All Around the Table)" (1987) and "Son of Scotty and Stuart" (1994). She also held a role in Richard Foreman's 1981 avant-garde drama film "Strong Medicine." Earlier in her career, she was recognized as a painter, with a recorded exhibition of her work at the Visual Arts Center of New Jersey in 1962. A fixture of the downtown Manhattan arts community, she lived at Patchin Place in Greenwich Village and was associated with notable figures such as painter George Deem and dance historian Sally Banes, the latter of whom described her participation in experimental film and performance contexts.

segment.text: exhibition programming between November 1968 and February 1969.
